answer: In Cynewulf's Elene (work slug: cynewulf-elene-d2d132, chapter 1), the True Cross is identified by raising a dead man in the following way: after Judas digs up three crosses at Calvary and brings them before Queen Elene, no one can tell which is the True Cross. Judas commands the three roods to be set in the middle of Jerusalem and the assembly waits until the ninth hour. A funeral procession then passes — a dead youth carried on a bier. Judas has the corpse laid on the ground and raises the first two crosses over it one at a time; the body remains dead. When he raises the third cross — 'the prince's Rood ... the Sky-King's beam, the true victory-beacon' — the youth instantly revives: 'He at once arose, with spirit endowed, together both body and soul.' This miracle identifies the third cross as the True Cross. The passage occurs in paragraph p-f5d1f1 of chapter 1.
